---
title: "Embeddings & Vector Search Internals"
description: "Similarity metrics, brute-force NN, IVF, PQ, and HNSW — implemented from scratch, measured on a real corpus."
author: "Prerna Tyagi"
date: "2026-05-27"
date-modified: last-modified
categories: [retrieval, embeddings, ann]
draft: true
reading-time: true
toc: true
toc-depth: 3
code-fold: false
---

::: {.callout-note}
**Draft scaffold.** Section headers and placeholders only. Content gets written from notes + experiments in [ml-applied-depth/blog/01-embeddings](https://github.com/tyagiprerna0808/ml-applied-depth/tree/main/blog/01-embeddings). Remove this callout and set `draft: false` to publish.
:::

## The question

> Given a corpus of millions of documents and a query, return the top-k nearest neighbors in <10 ms with recall@10 > 0.95, on a laptop. How does that actually work?

*[Set up the problem. Why naive search hits a wall. What "approximate" means and the tradeoff axes: recall, latency, build time, memory.]*

## 1. What an embedding actually is

*[The basis-free view. Intrinsic vs extrinsic geometry. Why "similarity" is a choice — show the same embedding compared by cosine vs dot product producing different rankings on the same query.]*

## 2. Similarity metrics

*[Cosine, dot product, L2. When each is correct. The unit-normalization trick — what it costs (and the cases where it isn't free).]*

## 3. Brute-force baseline

*[Naive O(n) search. Vectorized numpy. Plot: runtime vs n at fixed d, runtime vs d at fixed n. The point where brute-force stops being acceptable.]*

## 4. The curse of dimensionality

*[Empirical: distribution of pairwise distances at d=10, 100, 1000. Plot. Explain what the concentration means for nearest-neighbor algorithms.]*

## 5. IVF — Inverted File Index

*[k-means clustering of the corpus. Probe count. Recall/latency tradeoff. From-scratch implementation. Plot: recall@10 vs nprobe at fixed nlist; recall@10 vs nlist at fixed nprobe.]*

## 6. PQ — Product Quantization

*[Block decomposition. Codebook learning. Asymmetric distance computation. From-scratch implementation. Memory footprint comparison. Plot.]*

## 7. HNSW — Hierarchical Navigable Small World

*[Multi-layer graph construction. Greedy descent. M and efSearch. The small-world property derivation, not assertion. From-scratch implementation. Plots: recall vs efSearch, recall vs M.]*

## 8. Head-to-head

*[Single plot: recall@10 vs query latency for brute-force, IVF, PQ, HNSW, and FAISS reference. Build time vs N. Memory footprint vs N. The one chart that summarizes the whole post.]*

## 9. Production considerations

*[When to use what. Hosted vs in-process. Filtered search (when metadata matters). Updating the index. The decisions that don't show up in benchmarks.]*

## What surprised me

*[The bugs hit. The configs that didn't work. The number that came out smaller/larger than expected. This section is the credibility signal — keep it specific and honest.]*

## Build it yourself

The companion notebook in [ml-applied-depth/blog/01-embeddings/assignment.ipynb](https://github.com/tyagiprerna0808/ml-applied-depth/tree/main/blog/01-embeddings) has the same content as scaffolding with TODOs and tests. Implement each section yourself before reading the worked version in `notebook.ipynb`.

## References

- Malkov & Yashunin, 2016 — *Efficient and robust approximate nearest neighbor search using Hierarchical Navigable Small World graphs*
- Jégou, Douze, Schmid, 2010 — *Product quantization for nearest neighbor search*
- Johnson, Douze, Jégou, 2017 — *Billion-scale similarity search with GPUs* (FAISS)
- FAISS wiki — Getting Started + Index Types
